summary: DHH traces the redemption arcs of tech companies he once opposed, focusing on Microsoft and Meta. Microsoft transformed from the sworn enemy of open source in the 1990s to one of its biggest champions through GitHub, VSCode, and WSL. Meta, through Zuckerberg's embrace of open-source AI with Llama 3, now serves as a critical counterweight to closed AI cartels and government-controlled content moderation. DHH argues that self-interest, not altruism, drives these positive shifts, invoking Adam Smith to make the case that competition and free markets matter more than trusting any single company's motives. He concludes that defending free markets, free speech, and open AI outweighs his earlier concerns about targeted advertising.
key_points: Microsoft completed a remarkable villain-to-hero arc by becoming a major open source sponsor, steward of GitHub, and creator of VSCode and WSL
Meta's open-source release of Llama 3 is a vital counterbalance to closed AI development and government-industry regulatory capture
The fusion of state power and platform censorship through 'fact checkers' and content moderation poses a greater threat than targeted advertising
Self-interest and market competition, not altruism, are the reliable forces that produce good outcomes — per Adam Smith
Defending free markets for AI, speech, and ideas should be prioritized over holding grudges against former tech villains
money_quotes: They've gone from being the sworn enemy of open source to one of the biggest sponsors of it.

Zuckerberg's embrace of open source AI is an invaluable counter to the cartel-adjacent bullshit of 'AI safety & ethics' that would see the likes of OpenAI and Google conspire with governments around the world to determine what math should be allowed to predict the next token.
I've seen this movie before, and I'm not interested in a rerun.
Whatever trust I may once have had in objective third-party 'fact checkers' have long since evaporated from the catastrophic track record of these anything-but-neutral, would-be arbiters of truth.
We don't need altruism to save us, we need incentives. We need competition. We need free markets for products, platforms, services, ideas, speech, and AI.
We need to put the locus of control with consumers and individuals, not bureaucrats and monopolists.
I'm more than willing to commute the sentencing of former villains who've served their time and changed their minds.
key_insight: Tech companies should be judged by their current alignment with open markets and open source, not by past sins — and self-interested competition, not regulation or altruism, is the most reliable guardian of freedom.
